steps.title: Meet the Ellipse
steps.introText: An ellipse is defined by two fixed points called foci. It is the set of all points where the sum of the distances to the two foci stays constant — like a loop of string pinned at the foci.
steps.prompt: Drag the two foci apart and together: the string length (d₁ + d₂) stays fixed, so spreading them flattens the ellipse and bringing them together rounds it. Drag the point on the curve to check that d₁ + d₂ is the same everywhere, or use the a handle to resize.

steps.title: Axes and the Equation
steps.goal: Connect the axes to the standard equation.
steps.introText: An ellipse that is centered at the origin has an equation of the form x²/a² + y²/b² = 1. Here, the parameter a is the horizontal semi-axis and the parameter is the vertical semi-axis. A semi-axis is similar to the radius of a circle, which we can see in the graph below. When a = b, the two semi-axes are equal and the ellipse is just a circle.
steps.prompt: Drag the a and b handles to try three different shapes. Watch how a², b², and the equation change.

steps.title: Finding the Foci
steps.goal: Use c = √(a² − b²) to locate the foci.
steps.introText: The foci sit on the major axis, a distance c from the center. To find c, subtract the squares of the semi-axes and take the square root: c = √(a² − b²).
steps.prompt: Drag the a and b handles and watch the foci move. The green segment is c — the distance from the center to each focus — and the readout updates c = √(a² − b²) as you go.

steps.title: Match the Equation
steps.goal: Determine the major and minor axes of an ellipse based on its equation.
steps.prompt: Build the ellipse x²/25 + y²/9 = 1 (centered at the origin), then press Check.

feedback.correct: Perfect! √25 = 5 and √9 = 3, so a = 5 and b = 3.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet — the denominators are a² and b². Take their square roots.
feedback.hint: a² = 25 → a = 5; b² = 9 → b = 3. Stretch the a and b handles to match.

steps.title: Where Are the Foci?
steps.goal: Locate the foci using c² = a² − b².
steps.prompt: Consider the ellipse you just graph: centered at the origin with a = 5 (horizontal) and b = 3. Using what you learned previously, where are its foci? If you are stuck, you can also return to the previous step and use the graph to help you.
steps.feedback: Right — c² = 25 − 9 = 16, so c = 4 and the foci sit on the major (horizontal) axis at (±4, 0).

steps.title: Shifted Ellipse
steps.goal: Be able to graph an ellipse that is not centered at the origin.
steps.info: An ellipse that is not centered at the origin has an equation of the form (x − h)²/a² + (y − k)²/b² = 1. Here, the parameters and a and b are the same as before, but we have two new parameters h and k. The center of the ellipse is at (h, k).

feedback.correct: Nice! (x + 3) means h = −3 and (y − 2) means k = 2, with a = √16 = 4 and b = √4 = 2.
feedback.incorrect: Check the center and both axes: (x + 3) gives h = −3, (y − 2) gives k = 2.
feedback.hint: Move the center to (−3, 2), then set a = 4 and b = 2.

steps.title: Check Your Understanding
steps.prompt: Which statement is always true for an ellipse?

choices.label: The sum of the distances from any point to the two foci is constant.
The two semi-axes a and b are always equal.
The foci lie outside the ellipse.
An ellipse is defined by a single focus.

steps.feedback: Correct — that constant-sum property is the definition. When a = b the foci merge at the center and the ellipse becomes a circle.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Build three ellipses with the given size and position — no hints.

ellipseSequence.label: Wide ellipse at the origin (a = 6, b = 3)
Tall ellipse at the origin (a = 3, b = 5)
Ellipse centered at (−2, 1) with a = 4, b = 2

steps.completionMessage: Congratulations! You can now read, build, and shift ellipses using a, b, and the focus relationship c² = a² − b².
